name: Montenegro
tagline: A mountain stronghold that defied an empire, lost its crown to a union, and won back its statehood by a single percentage point.
summary: Montenegro's history is the story of a small Balkan people that built and rebuilt its sovereignty against far greater powers. Its medieval root was the South Slavic principality of Duklja, later known as Zeta, which produced the first crowned king of the region in the eleventh century. As Ottoman power overwhelmed the Balkans, a remnant of free highlanders gathered around the Orthodox see of Cetinje, and from 1696 the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty turned this theocratic Prince-Bishopric, or Vladika, into a famous mountain redoubt of armed resistance to the Ottoman Empire, immortalised by the poet-prince Petar II Petrovic-Njegos. In 1852 the bishopric became a secular principality under Danilo; Montenegro's full independence was recognised by the Great Powers at the Congress of Berlin in 1878, and in 1910 Nikola I raised it to a kingdom. After the First World War the contested Podgorica Assembly of 1918 deposed the dynasty and merged Montenegro into the new Yugoslav state, dividing the nation between unionist "Whites" and pro-independence "Greens". For most of the twentieth century Montenegro was a Yugoslav republic; it remained with Serbia in the rump Federal Republic of Yugoslavia and the State Union of Serbia and Montenegro, until the narrow independence referendum of 21 May 2006 restored the sovereign state. Independent Montenegro joined NATO in 2017 and pursues European Union membership.

founding.label: Independence recognised at the Congress of Berlin
founding.detail: Montenegro had been effectively self-governing in its mountains for generations, but its sovereignty was first recognised in international law at the Congress of Berlin in 1878. Article XXVI of the Treaty of Berlin, signed on 13 July 1878, declared that 'the independence of Montenegro is recognized by the Sublime Porte and by all those of the High Contracting Parties who had not hitherto admitted it,' ending Ottoman suzerainty. The settlement also enlarged the principality and granted it the Adriatic port of Antivari (Bar), making Montenegro the twenty-seventh recognised independent state of its era. Montenegrins themselves trace the modern restoration of that status directly to 1878: when the country voted to leave the State Union with Serbia in 2006, official statements described the act as regaining the international standing first recognised at the Congress of Berlin.

quickFacts.label: Medieval root
quickFacts.value: The South Slavic principality of Duklja, later Zeta; the name Montenegro appears under the Crnojevic dynasty in the late 15th century

quickFacts.label: Theocratic state
quickFacts.value: Prince-Bishopric (Vladika) of Montenegro under the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty, hereditary from 1696, centred on Cetinje

quickFacts.label: Independence recognised
quickFacts.value: Congress of Berlin, 13 July 1878 (Article XXVI of the Treaty of Berlin)

quickFacts.label: Kingdom
quickFacts.value: Proclaimed by Prince Nikola I on 28 August 1910

quickFacts.label: Lost statehood
quickFacts.value: Podgorica Assembly deposed the dynasty and united Montenegro with Serbia, 1918

quickFacts.label: Independence restored
quickFacts.value: Referendum of 21 May 2006 (55.5% for independence); declared 3 June 2006

eras.title: Duklja, Zeta and the Coming of the Name Montenegro
eras.standfirst: A South Slavic principality on the Adriatic produced the region's first crowned king, passed into the Serbian realm as Zeta, and under its last dynasty took the name by which the country is still known.
body: The earliest medieval state on the territory of modern Montenegro was Duklja, a South Slavic principality stretching from the Bay of Kotor to the river Bojana and inland to the sources of the Zeta and Moraca. In 1042 Stefan Vojislav led a successful revolt that secured Duklja's independence from the Byzantine Empire and founded the Vojislavljevic dynasty. His successor Mihailo became the first ruler of the region to bear the title of king, the honour bestowed on him in 1077 by Pope Gregory VII, head of the Roman Catholic Church.

From the twelfth century Duklja was drawn into the orbit of the rising Serbian state, becoming a crown land of the Grand Principality of Serbia under the Nemanjic dynasty and increasingly known as Zeta. After the disintegration of the Serbian Empire in the later fourteenth century, an independent Principality of Zeta re-emerged, ruled by the House of Balsic between 1356 and 1421 and then by the House of Crnojevic from 1431. It was under the Crnojevic that the name Montenegro, the 'Black Mountain', came into use for the country in the late fifteenth century.

The Crnojevic capital at Cetinje, established in the harsh mountain interior as Ottoman pressure grew, became the seat of both princely power and the Orthodox metropolitanate. There Djurdje Crnojevic, ruler of Zeta, founded the first printing house among the South Slavs: a press with movable type acquired in Venice produced, on 4 January 1494, the Oktoih (Octoechos), the first Cyrillic book printed in southeastern Europe. The press closed around 1496 when Crnojevic left the country before the advancing Ottomans, who by the end of the century had reduced Zeta to a remnant clinging to the mountains.

events.title: Duklja wins independence from Byzantium
events.summary: Stefan Vojislav led a revolt that freed the principality of Duklja from Byzantine control and established the Vojislavljevic dynasty, the first medieval state on the territory of Montenegro.

events.title: Mihailo crowned king of Duklja
events.summary: Mihailo of Duklja became the first ruler of the region to bear the royal title, the crown bestowed on him by Pope Gregory VII, marking Duklja's standing among Christian states.

events.title: Principality of Zeta under the Balsic
events.summary: After the fall of the Serbian Empire an independent Zeta emerged, ruled by the House of Balsic and then from 1431 by the House of Crnojevic, under whom the name Montenegro came into use.

events.title: The Oktoih and the Crnojevic printing press
events.summary: Djurdje Crnojevic founded the first South Slavic printing house at Cetinje, which produced the Oktoih, the first Cyrillic book printed in southeastern Europe, before closing around 1496 amid the Ottoman advance.

figures.name: Mihailo of Duklja
figures.role: First king of the medieval principality
figures.blurb: Ruler of Duklja who in 1077 received a royal crown from Pope Gregory VII, becoming the first king of the South Slavic state that lay at the root of Montenegro.

figures.name: Djurdje Crnojevic
figures.role: Ruler of Zeta and founder of the first Slavic press
figures.blurb: The last Crnojevic ruler of Zeta, who established at Cetinje the first printing house among the South Slavs; its Oktoih of 1494 was the first Cyrillic book printed in southeastern Europe.

pullquote.text: Under the Crnojevic dynasty the country took the name Montenegro, the Black Mountain, and at Cetinje produced the first printed book among the South Slavs.
pullquote.attribution: On the late medieval principality of Zeta

eras.title: The Prince-Bishopric and the Petrovic-Njegos Resistance
eras.standfirst: A free remnant of highlanders gathered around the see of Cetinje and became a theocratic state whose hereditary prince-bishops led a long armed resistance to the Ottoman Empire.
body: As Ottoman power swept over the Balkans, the inhabitants of the Montenegrin highlands preserved a fierce autonomy that no neighbouring empire fully extinguished. Authority came to rest with the Orthodox bishops of Cetinje, later metropolitans, who from 1516 combined spiritual and temporal leadership in a theocratic Prince-Bishopric, the office of vladika. For nearly two centuries the position was elective, but in 1696 Metropolitan Danilo Petrovic-Njegos secured the right to nominate his own successor, founding the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty that would rule Montenegro until 1918. Because Orthodox prelates were celibate, the title passed from uncle to nephew.

From its mountain stronghold the Prince-Bishopric became famous for armed defiance of the Ottoman Empire, raiding and being raided across a hostile frontier and cultivating ties with Orthodox Russia as a protector. Successive vladikas, among them Vasilije and the long-lived Petar I, who was later canonised, consolidated the tribes, codified rudimentary law and held the high country against repeated Ottoman expeditions. The state's survival depended less on formal institutions than on the warrior ethos of the clans and the unifying authority of the bishop-prince at Cetinje.

The most celebrated of the prince-bishops was Petar II Petrovic-Njegos, who ruled from 1830 to 1851 and was at once a reforming statesman and the greatest poet of his language. He strengthened central authority, founded schools and a printing press, and in 1847 published The Mountain Wreath (Gorski vijenac), a dramatic epic set in earlier Montenegro that became a national masterpiece. Revered as the country's most illustrious son, Njegos embodied the fusion of clergy, sovereignty and poetry that defined the theocratic age, which ended soon after his death.

events.title: Rise of the theocratic Prince-Bishopric
events.summary: Authority over the free Montenegrin highlands passed to the Orthodox bishops of Cetinje, who combined spiritual and temporal power as vladikas in a theocratic state that renounced Ottoman overlordship.

events.title: Petrovic-Njegos dynasty established
events.summary: Metropolitan Danilo Petrovic-Njegos secured the right to name his successor, founding the hereditary Petrovic-Njegos line of prince-bishops that ruled Montenegro until 1918, the title passing from uncle to nephew.

events.title: Rule of Petar II Petrovic-Njegos
events.summary: The poet-prince Petar II strengthened the state, founded schools and reformed taxation, and wrote The Mountain Wreath, becoming Montenegro's most celebrated cultural figure.

events.title: Publication of The Mountain Wreath
events.summary: Njegos published Gorski vijenac (The Mountain Wreath), a dramatic epic in verse set in eighteenth-century Montenegro that became a defining work of the national literature.

figures.name: Danilo I Petrovic-Njegos
figures.blurb: Metropolitan of Cetinje who in 1696 founded the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty by securing the right to nominate his successor, anchoring the theocratic state and its resistance to the Ottomans.

figures.name: Petar II Petrovic-Njegos
figures.blurb: Vladika of Montenegro from 1830, a reforming ruler and the greatest poet of his language, whose 1847 epic The Mountain Wreath made him the most revered figure in Montenegrin culture.

pullquote.text: From the mountain see of Cetinje the prince-bishops of the Petrovic-Njegos line led a celebrated resistance to the Ottoman Empire, the office passing from uncle to nephew.

eras.title: Secular Principality, the Congress of Berlin and the Kingdom
eras.standfirst: Danilo turned the bishopric into a secular principality; international recognition came at the Congress of Berlin in 1878, and in 1910 Nikola I crowned himself king.
body: In 1852 Danilo Petrovic-Njegos renounced the ecclesiastical office, married and transformed Montenegro from a theocracy into a secular principality, taking the title of prince. His 1855 legal code, often called Danilo's Code, gave the new state its first modern legal framework and ended centuries of rule by bishop-princes. Danilo's reign was cut short by his assassination in 1860, and he was succeeded by his nephew Nikola, who would dominate Montenegrin politics for more than half a century.

Montenegro's long struggle with the Ottoman Empire culminated in the Balkan crisis of the 1870s. Fighting alongside Russia and Serbia in the Russo-Turkish War, the principality won territory on the battlefield, and its sovereignty was formally enshrined at the Congress of Berlin. Article XXVI of the Treaty of Berlin of 13 July 1878 recognised Montenegro's independence, ending Ottoman suzerainty, while Article XXIX enlarged the country and granted it the Adriatic port of Antivari (Bar), though it imposed restrictions barring Montenegro from maintaining a navy or a war flag.

Under Prince Nikola I, Montenegro modernised its institutions and expanded, adopting its first constitution in 1905. On 28 August 1910, marking the fiftieth year of his reign, Nikola proclaimed Montenegro a kingdom and assumed the title of king, making it the last of the Balkan monarchies. In the First Balkan War of 1912-1913 Montenegro joined its neighbours in driving the Ottomans from most of their remaining European lands, capturing the city of Scutari, though the Great Powers compelled it to relinquish that prize.

events.title: Montenegro becomes a secular principality
events.summary: Danilo renounced the office of prince-bishop, married and proclaimed himself prince, ending the theocracy; his 1855 code gave the principality its first modern legal framework.

events.title: Independence recognised at the Congress of Berlin
events.summary: The Treaty of Berlin recognised Montenegro's independence under Article XXVI, ending Ottoman suzerainty, and under Article XXIX enlarged the state and granted it the port of Antivari (Bar).

events.title: First Montenegrin constitution
events.summary: Prince Nikola I granted Montenegro its first constitution, establishing an assembly and modern institutions as the principality consolidated its recognised statehood.

events.title: Montenegro proclaimed a kingdom
events.summary: On the fiftieth anniversary of his reign, Prince Nikola I proclaimed Montenegro a kingdom and assumed the title of king, the last monarchy created in the Balkans.

events.title: The Balkan Wars
events.summary: Montenegro joined Serbia, Bulgaria and Greece in the First Balkan War against the Ottoman Empire and besieged Scutari, but was forced by the Great Powers to give up the captured city.

figures.name: Danilo I, Prince of Montenegro
figures.blurb: The vladika who in 1852 secularised the state and became prince, issuing the 1855 legal code; his assassination in 1860 brought his nephew Nikola to power.

figures.name: Nikola I
figures.role: Prince and then King of Montenegro
figures.blurb: Ruler for nearly six decades who won recognition of independence in 1878, granted a constitution in 1905 and crowned himself king in 1910, the last sovereign of an independent Montenegrin monarchy.

pullquote.text: The independence of Montenegro is recognized by the Sublime Porte and by all those of the High Contracting Parties who had not hitherto admitted it.

eras.title: War, the Podgorica Assembly and Yugoslav Montenegro
eras.standfirst: Occupied in the First World War, Montenegro was merged into Serbia and Yugoslavia by the disputed Podgorica Assembly of 1918, splitting the nation before a wartime rebirth as a Yugoslav republic.
body: Montenegro entered the First World War as an ally of Serbia. In January 1916, after the Montenegrin army won the celebrated Battle of Mojkovac to cover the retreat of the Serbs, the kingdom was overrun and occupied by Austria-Hungary; King Nikola I and his government fled into exile by way of Italy to France. The country was liberated in 1918, but its fate was decided not by its exiled monarch but by an assembly convened in Podgorica.

The Podgorica Assembly, meeting in late November 1918, deposed King Nikola I and the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty and proclaimed the unconditional union of Montenegro with Serbia within the new Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes. The decision split Montenegrin society between the 'Whites' (Bjelasi), who favoured unification with Serbia, and the 'Greens' (Zelenasi), who wanted Montenegro to enter the union as an equal partner or to keep its independence. Pro-independence forces launched the Christmas Uprising in January 1919, but it was suppressed, and the manner of the union remained bitterly contested for the rest of the century.

Within royal Yugoslavia Montenegro lost its separate statehood. During the Second World War it was occupied and became a centre of partisan resistance, and in socialist Yugoslavia it was reconstituted in 1945 as the Socialist Republic of Montenegro, one of the six federal republics, with Titograd (modern Podgorica) as its capital. For four decades Montenegro existed as a Yugoslav republic, regaining a recognised political identity within the federation even as full sovereignty remained a memory of the kingdom abolished in 1918.

events.title: Battle of Mojkovac and Austro-Hungarian occupation
events.summary: The Montenegrin army won the Battle of Mojkovac (7-8 January 1916) to cover the Serbian retreat, but the kingdom was overrun and occupied by Austria-Hungary, and King Nikola I fled into exile.

events.title: The Podgorica Assembly
events.summary: An assembly in Podgorica deposed King Nikola I and the Petrovic-Njegos dynasty and proclaimed the unconditional union of Montenegro with Serbia in the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes.

events.title: The Christmas Uprising and the Greens-Whites split
events.summary: Pro-independence 'Greens' rose against the union proclaimed at Podgorica but were defeated by the unionist 'Whites', a schism over Montenegro's statehood that endured for generations.

events.title: Socialist Republic of Montenegro
events.summary: After wartime occupation and partisan resistance, Montenegro became one of the six federal republics of socialist Yugoslavia, with its capital at Titograd, today's Podgorica.

figures.name: Nikola I in exile
figures.blurb: Driven from his kingdom by the Austro-Hungarian occupation in 1916, Nikola I was deposed in absentia by the Podgorica Assembly in 1918 and died in exile in France in 1921, the dynasty's rule ended.

figures.name: Janko Vukotic
figures.blurb: Serdar who led the Montenegrin army to victory at the Battle of Mojkovac in January 1916, shielding the retreating Serbian forces in one of the country's most celebrated military feats.

pullquote.text: The Podgorica Assembly of 1918 deposed the dynasty and united Montenegro with Serbia, opening a lasting division between unionist Whites and pro-independence Greens.
pullquote.attribution: On the loss of Montenegrin statehood

eras.title: Rump Yugoslavia, the State Union and the Road to the 2006 Referendum
eras.standfirst: As Yugoslavia broke apart, Montenegro stayed with Serbia in a rump federation and then a loose State Union, before a narrow referendum in 2006 restored independence.
body: When Yugoslavia disintegrated in the early 1990s, Montenegro alone chose to remain with Serbia. In 1992 the two republics formed the Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, a rump state that endured the wars, sanctions and isolation of the decade. By the turn of the century Montenegro's leadership had distanced itself from Belgrade, even adopting the German mark and then, from 2002, the euro as its sole legal tender, a unilateral euroisation undertaken without the agreement of the European Central Bank.

Under European mediation the federation was reorganised in 2003 into a loose State Union of Serbia and Montenegro. Its Constitutional Charter included a crucial provision: after three years either republic could hold a referendum on independence. As that period elapsed, the European Union set the terms of any vote, requiring that independence be approved by at least 55 percent of valid votes, a qualified majority that critics argued had been fixed for political reasons.

The referendum was held on 21 May 2006. With turnout above 86 percent, 55.5 percent of voters chose independence, narrowly clearing the 55 percent threshold. International observers from the OSCE Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights concluded that the vote 'was conducted in line with OSCE and other international standards related to democratic electoral processes' and gave citizens 'a genuine opportunity to determine the future course of Montenegro as an independent state.' On 3 June 2006 the parliament in Podgorica formally declared independence, restoring the sovereign Montenegrin state that had been lost in 1918.

events.title: Federal Republic of Yugoslavia
events.summary: As the other republics broke away, Montenegro joined Serbia in forming the rump Federal Republic of Yugoslavia, remaining bound to Belgrade through the wars and sanctions of the 1990s.

events.title: Unilateral adoption of the euro
events.summary: Montenegro made the euro its sole legal tender from March 2002, a unilateral euroisation undertaken without European Central Bank agreement that distanced its economy from Serbia's.

events.title: State Union of Serbia and Montenegro
events.summary: European mediation transformed the federation into the loose State Union of Serbia and Montenegro, whose Constitutional Charter allowed either republic to hold an independence referendum after three years.

events.title: Independence referendum
events.summary: On a turnout above 86 percent, 55.5 percent of voters chose independence, narrowly passing the EU-set 55 percent threshold; the OSCE/ODIHR found the vote met international standards.

events.title: Restoration of independence
events.summary: The parliament in Podgorica formally declared Montenegro an independent sovereign state, recognised by Serbia, restoring the statehood lost at the Podgorica Assembly of 1918.

figures.name: Milo Djukanovic
figures.blurb: Dominant Montenegrin politician who steered the republic away from Belgrade, championed the 2006 independence referendum and led the country's bids for NATO and EU membership.

figures.name: Javier Solana
figures.blurb: European Union foreign-policy chief whose mediation produced the 2003 State Union of Serbia and Montenegro and whose office set the 55 percent threshold for the 2006 referendum.

pullquote.text: On a turnout above 86 percent, 55.5 percent of voters chose independence, narrowly clearing the 55 percent threshold set by the European Union.

eras.title: Independent Montenegro, NATO and Europe
eras.standfirst: The restored state pursued Euro-Atlantic integration, joining NATO in 2017 and negotiating EU membership, while working to settle its borders with Croatia and Kosovo.
body: Independent Montenegro set its course toward Euro-Atlantic integration. It applied for European Union membership in 2008 and was granted candidate status in December 2010, opening accession negotiations that have made it one of the front-runners among the Western Balkan states. The country retained the euro as its currency, continuing the unilateral euroisation it had begun in 2002, a status it shares in Europe only with Kosovo.

On 5 June 2017 Montenegro became the twenty-ninth member of NATO, depositing its instrument of accession in Washington after the parliaments of all the existing allies had ratified its membership. NATO's Secretary General declared that the country joined 'with a seat at the table as an equal, with an equal voice in shaping our Alliance, and its independence guaranteed,' a step taken despite strong opposition from Russia and from parts of the domestic public.

Two border questions accompanied the new state's diplomacy. With Croatia, the status of the Prevlaka peninsula at the entrance to the Bay of Kotor and the surrounding maritime boundary remained unresolved, the two sides agreeing interim arrangements while leaving the demarcation to future negotiation or international adjudication. With Kosovo, a demarcation agreement for the 79-kilometre border was signed in 2015 but proved deeply controversial in Pristina, where opposition parties charged that it ceded land; after years of protest it was finally ratified by Kosovo's parliament in March 2018, the last major piece of Montenegro's frontier settlement.

events.title: Application for EU membership
events.summary: Montenegro applied to join the European Union, beginning the accession process that would make it one of the leading candidates among the Western Balkan countries.

events.title: EU candidate status
events.summary: The European Union granted Montenegro candidate status, a milestone toward the accession negotiations the country has pursued while retaining the euro as its currency.

events.title: Kosovo border demarcation
events.summary: Montenegro and Kosovo signed a demarcation of their 79-kilometre border in 2015; fiercely contested in Kosovo, it was finally ratified by Pristina's parliament in March 2018.

events.title: Accession to NATO
events.summary: Montenegro became NATO's twenty-ninth member, depositing its instrument of accession in Washington, despite strong Russian opposition and a divided domestic public.

figures.name: Dusko Markovic
figures.blurb: Prime minister who led Montenegro into NATO in June 2017, attending the accession ceremony in Washington as the country completed its principal Euro-Atlantic goal.

figures.name: Jens Stoltenberg
figures.blurb: Secretary General who welcomed Montenegro as the Alliance's twenty-ninth member in 2017, hailing its entry 'with a seat at the table as an equal' and its independence guaranteed.

pullquote.text: Today, Montenegro joins NATO with a seat at the table as an equal, with an equal voice in shaping our Alliance, and its independence guaranteed.
pullquote.attribution: NATO Secretary General Jens Stoltenberg, 5 June 2017

figures.name: Petar II Petrovic-Njegos
figures.blurb: The most revered figure in Montenegrin culture: vladika of the theocratic Prince-Bishopric, reforming ruler and author of the 1847 epic The Mountain Wreath.

figures.name: Danilo I, Prince of Montenegro
figures.blurb: Ended the theocracy in 1852 by becoming prince, transforming the Prince-Bishopric into a secular principality and issuing its first modern legal code.

figures.name: Nikola I
figures.blurb: Ruler for nearly six decades who won recognition of independence in 1878 and proclaimed Montenegro a kingdom in 1910, before being deposed by the Podgorica Assembly in 1918.

figures.name: Milo Djukanovic
figures.blurb: The dominant politician of independent Montenegro, who led the 2006 referendum that restored statehood and the drives for NATO and EU membership.
